question: Describe the primary role of a staging area in a data warehousing architecture.
expectedPoints: Serves as a temporary holding area for raw data extracted from source systems.
Acts as a buffer to isolate the performance impact of data extraction on source systems.
Provides a location for initial data cleansing and transformation before loading into the data warehouse.

question: Explain the concept of a data mart and describe two different approaches to building one.
expectedPoints: A data mart is a subset of a data warehouse, focused on a specific subject area or department.
Top-down approach: Built from an existing enterprise data warehouse, ensuring consistency.
Bottom-up approach: Built as an independent, standalone system, often for quicker deployment.

question: Compare and contrast the Kimball and Inmon approaches to data warehousing, focusing on their core methodologies, structural differences, and typical use cases.
expectedPoints: Kimball (Bottom-up): Starts with departmental data marts built around fact tables and conformed dimensions, which are later integrated into a data warehouse bus architecture.
Inmon (Top-down): Starts with a single, integrated, normalized enterprise data warehouse (EDW) from which data marts are derived as logical subsets.
Kimball is often faster to implement for specific business needs, while Inmon provides a more holistic, enterprise-wide view but is more complex and time-consuming to build.
